content.body: Patient confidentiality is not merely a legal obligation — it is the foundation of the trust that makes healthcare possible. When a patient hands you a prescription, they are trusting you with intimate details of their health. They are trusting that you will not share their medical conditions, medications, or personal information with anyone who does not have a legitimate need to know. A single breach of this trust can destroy a patient's willingness to seek treatment, with potentially fatal consequences.

Breaching patient confidentiality can result in: termination of employment, criminal prosecution under data protection laws, civil lawsuits for damages, removal from any professional register, and most importantly — real harm to the patient whose privacy you violated. In small Caribbean communities, a confidentiality breach can lead to social stigma, discrimination, damaged relationships, and patients avoiding necessary treatment.
content.title: Confidentiality Is Not Optional

content.body: ALL patient information is confidential. This includes, but is not limited to:

items: The patient's name and the fact that they visited the pharmacy at all
Every medication they are prescribed or purchase
Their medical conditions, diagnoses, and health history
Their address, date of birth, and contact information
Anything the patient tells you verbally during counselling
The prescriber's identity and the contents of the prescription
Any notes in the patient's pharmacy file

rows: Discussing a patient's medication within earshot of others, gossiping about patients with colleagues, calling out a patient's name and medication loudly at the pickup counter
Lower your voice, use private counselling areas, never discuss patients outside the pharmacy
Leaving prescriptions visible on the counter, prescription bags with visible medication names, computer screens displaying patient records visible to the public
Position screens away from public view, use privacy screens, handle prescriptions face-down
Sharing patient records via unsecured email, leaving pharmacy systems logged in unattended, storing patient data on personal devices, sharing patient information on WhatsApp or social media
Log out when stepping away, never use personal devices for patient data, use only approved communication channels
Improperly disposing of prescriptions or patient records (not shredded), leaving patient files accessible to unauthorised staff
Shred all documents containing patient information, lock filing cabinets, restrict access to records

content.body: For a pharmacy technician in the Caribbean, confidentiality and data protection are both professional obligations and legal requirements. You must safeguard all patient information you handle, whether in paper or digital form, and ensure it is only disclosed when permitted by law, patient consent, or a public interest need.

items: Respect patient privacy: Treat all identifiable patient information as sensitive and protect it from unauthorized access Medical Protection.
Secure storage: Keep records in cabinets or use password-protected systems/secure digital environments. Avoid leaving documents unattended or in public areas.
Limit access: Only share information with authorized staff or healthcare providers who need it for treatment, payment, or operations.
Avoid overheard conversations: Ensure consultations or discussions about patients are not overheard.

Caribbean context: While privacy laws vary, many CARICOM member states (e.g., Jamaica, Trinidad and Tobago) have enacted data protection laws influenced by international standards like the EU GDPR ResearchGate. Even in countries without a specific privacy law, professional codes require confidentiality.
GDPR/GDPR-like rules: If your pharmacy processes personal data, you must have a lawful basis for processing (e.g., compliance with a legal obligation, patient consent) and document it.
Professional codes: Pharmacy technician standards require adherence to confidentiality, integrity, and availability of patient information.
Retention policies: Follow prescription retention policies as legally regulated Archive any outdated records (e.g., after patient death or no longer needed)
Training: Ensure all staff are trained on confidentiality and data protection rules.
Incident reporting: Report any suspected breaches to your Pharmacist promptly.

content.body: In many Caribbean pharmacies, WhatsApp is informally used to communicate between staff, prescribers, and sometimes patients. This practice poses SERIOUS data protection risks. Patient information should NEVER be shared via WhatsApp or similar messaging platforms unless the system has been formally approved by the pharmacy's data protection policy and meets encryption and data residency requirements. A screenshot of a WhatsApp message containing patient information could be shared endlessly and constitutes a data breach.
content.title: WhatsApp Warning
